preamble: The glottal stop, a consonant produced by briefly closing the vocal cords, can undergo a phonetic transformation to a voiceless uvular plosive. This shift involves a change in the primary place of articulation from the glottis to the uvula, resulting in a sound produced further back in the mouth.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves a posteriorization or uvularization of the stop consonant, moving the place of articulation from glottal to uvular while maintaining the plosive manner

examples.note: Shift observed in Jewish varieties of Arabic in the Meghreb, influenced by the spread of koineized Muslim varieties.
Variation and shift between /ʔ/ and /q/ among speakers of Arabic dialects, such as in Bethlehem, where certain social groups may favor /q/ over /ʔ/.

Historical reconstruction suggests that the Phoenician letter qoph, which may have originally represented a glottal stop [ʔ], changed its phonetic value to a uvular plosive [q] when the alphabet spread to communities that pronounced [q].

sources: Modern Arabic Sociolinguistics: Diglossia, Variation, Codeswitching, Attitudes And Identity.
Elias, J. (2014). The Phoenician Alphabet Reassessed in Light of its Descendant Scripts and the Language of the Modern Lebanese.
